description: This figure contains two schematics of the air induction melting with flux cover (AIMFC) process for Fe-Al based lightweight alloy fabrication. Subplot (a) illustrates the melting stage setup: a 150 mm inner diameter, 300 mm tall crucible with MgO/Al₂O₃ lining and outer induction coils holds 240 mm deep molten Fe-Al alloy, covered by a 2-4 mm thick protective molten slag layer; a tundish and 80 mm inner diameter casting mould are also displayed as downstream equipment. Subplot (b) shows the pouring stage: the crucible tilts, with low-density molten slag remaining on top of the molten metal to isolate it from air, while the molten metal flows through the tundish into the mould and solidifies into porosity-free ingot. This cost-effective process avoids hydrogen pick-up from reactions between Al in the melt and atmospheric moisture, producing Fe-Al (7 wt.% and 9 wt.% Al) and Fe-Al-C alloys with comparable properties to vacuum induction melted products.

This plot displays the standard free energy of formation (ΔF°, unit: K.Cals) as a function of temperature (°C, ranging from 0°C to 2400°C) for two oxide systems, MgO (represented by open circles) and Al₂O₃ (represented by solid dots). Both oxides show an increasing trend of ΔF° (becoming less negative) as temperature rises. The two curves intersect at approximately 1600°C (≈1873K): below this temperature, Al₂O₃ has a more negative ΔF° (higher thermodynamic stability) than MgO, while above the intersection temperature, MgO is more stable. This thermodynamic data supports the conclusion that Al in molten Fe-Al alloys can reduce MgO crucible linings at temperatures close to the melting point of iron aluminides, leading to crucible erosion and Mg impurity pickup by the alloy, which guides appropriate crucible material selection for Fe-Al alloy melting.

This set of photos presents cold-rolled Fe-7 wt.% Al alloy samples manufactured via the air induction melting with flux cover (AIMFC) process. Subfigure (a) displays three flat cold-rolled plates and sheets with different sizes, while subfigure (b) shows four flexible rolled foils of varying dimensions, with a 2 cm scale bar in (b) for size reference. All samples exhibit intact macroscopic morphology without obvious visible defects, verifying the excellent cold workability of AIMFC-processed Fe-7 wt.% Al alloy, which can be processed into multiple product forms (plates, sheets, foils) to satisfy different application requirements. This figure supports the core conclusion that the cost-effective AIMFC process can produce workable Fe-7 wt.% Al lightweight steel with comparable performance to vacuum induction melted counterparts.

This set of optical micrographs presents the annealed microstructures of hot-rolled Fe-Al lightweight alloys fabricated via different melting routes. Subfigure (a) shows air induction melted (AIM) Fe-7 wt.% Al alloy, (b) shows flux-covered air induction melted (AIMFC) Fe-7 wt.% Al alloy, (c) shows AIMFC Fe-9 wt.% Al alloy, (d) shows vacuum induction melted (VIM) Fe-7 wt.% Al alloy, and (e) shows VIM Fe-9 wt.% Al alloy, with a 250 μm scale bar provided in subfigure (a). All samples were processed by hot forging and hot rolling (soaked at 1373 K, total thickness reduction of 50%) prior to microstructure observation. All samples exhibit coarse equiaxed single-phase α-Fe(Al) solid solution grains with a small number of dot-like pores or precipitates inside grains. The grain size of AIMFC-produced alloys is comparable to that of VIM-produced counterparts, and Fe-7 wt.% Al alloys have slightly smaller grain sizes than Fe-9 wt.% Al alloys. This figure verifies that the low-cost AIMFC process can achieve grain microstructure equivalent to that of the high-cost VIM process, supporting the feasibility of AIMFC for low-cost manufacturing of lightweight Fe-Al steels.

This set of optical micrographs presents the microstructure of hot-rolled air induction melted Fe-7 wt.% Al plates with different carbon contents. Subfigure (a) corresponds to 0.012 wt.% C, showing a single-phase equiaxed solid solution structure with almost no precipitates; subfigure (b) for 0.15 wt.% C has a small amount of dispersed fine precipitates; subfigure (c) for 0.35 wt.% C exhibits distinct Fe₃AlC₀.₅ precipitates distributed along grain boundaries; subfigure (d) for 0.5 wt.% C shows elongated precipitates aligned along the rolling direction; subfigure (e) for 1.0 wt.% C contains a high volume fraction of uniformly distributed fine precipitates, with a 250 μm scale bar provided. The results confirm that increasing carbon content significantly increases the volume fraction of Fe₃AlC₀.₅ precipitates and alters their morphology and distribution, supporting the conclusion that carbon addition regulates the mechanical properties of Fe-Al-C alloys through precipitation strengthening.

This figure presents two X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns collected using Cu-Kα radiation for Fe-7 wt.% Al alloys with different carbon contents. The first pattern, from Fe-7 wt.% Al-0.012 wt.% C alloy, only shows characteristic diffraction peaks of Fe₃Al phase, corresponding to (111), (220), (400), (422), and (440) crystal planes. The second pattern, from Fe-7 wt.% Al-0.15 wt.% C alloy, exhibits additional peaks attributed to Fe₃AlC₀.₅ phase, corresponding to its (200) and (311) crystal planes, besides the Fe₃Al peaks observed in the low-C sample. The results confirm that Fe₃AlC₀.₅ precipitates form when carbon content in Fe-7 wt.% Al alloy reaches 0.15 wt.% or higher, as similar peaks are also reported in alloys with 0.35–1 wt.% C, supporting the phase identification conclusion of the study.
